title: Bootstrap IPTVnator repository memory
summary: Shallow orientation memory for 4gray/iptvnator: Nx Angular/Electron workspace shape, documented commands, app routing and environment services, playlist persistence, SQLite schema, M3U state, Xtream/Stalker portal boundaries, and desktop downloads.

components.name: Angular web app shell and routes
Electron backend bootstrap and IPC handlers
Shared SQLite database schema and connection
Xtream portal feature and data access
Stalker portal feature and data access

flows.name: Build, serve, test, package, and release app targets
Start renderer shell and choose environment services
Import, persist, route, and play M3U playlists
Load Xtream portal catalog, user data, playback, and search
Load Stalker portal catalog, live TV, playback, favorites, and search
Queue, persist, retry, and play desktop downloads

claims.text: IPTVnator is an open-source IPTV player for M3U/M3U8 playlists, Xtream Codes, and Stalker portals, built as a cross-platform Angular and Electron application.
The application explicitly does not provide playlists or digital content; channels and screenshots are demonstration material only.

The repository is an Nx monorepo using pnpm 10.17.1 with applications for the Angular web app, Electron backend, Astro website, remote-control web app, and Xtream/Stalker mock servers plus shared libraries under libs/.
Documented local setup uses Corepack and pnpm; `pnpm run serve:backend` starts Electron with the Angular dev server at localhost:4200, while `pnpm run serve:frontend` serves only the Angular app.

Root scripts map common workflows to Nx targets: frontend and PWA builds, Electron backend build/serve, Jest tests for frontend and backend, Electron package/make targets, and Astro website serve/build.

The Angular route table redirects the root path to `/workspace`, then lazy-loads dashboard, sources, M3U playlist player, global favorites, downloads, Xtream routes, Stalker routes, and settings under the workspace shell.

App providers install NgRx playlist/router state, effects, i18n, IndexedDB, portal playback/navigation tokens, and Xtream data-source providers; `DataFactory` selects `ElectronService` when `window.electron` exists and `PwaService` otherwise.

`DataService` is the renderer-side abstraction for app version, environment, IPC send/listen, and listener cleanup; Electron and PWA implementations translate shared command names to native IPC or HTTP/proxy behavior.

Electron startup sets the app name, optionally relocates `userData`, initializes the database before registering Electron, playlist, shared, player, settings, Stalker, Xtream, database, EPG, and remote-control events, then resets stale downloads.

The preload script exposes one `window.electron` API for renderer access to playlist import/update, EPG operations, external players, portal requests, app settings, database CRUD, playback positions, downloads, and remote-control updates.

The shared Drizzle schema defines app playlists/state, Xtream categories and content, favorites, recently viewed items, EPG channels/programs, playback positions, and downloads, with foreign-key relationships back to playlists where relevant.
SQLite data defaults under `~/.iptvnator/databases/iptvnator.db`; `IPTVNATOR_E2E_DATA_DIR` redirects the data root and also gives Electron isolated user-data and config directories for tests.

`PlaylistsService` stores playlists through Electron SQLite APIs when the preload database methods exist, otherwise it uses browser IndexedDB; it also migrates legacy IndexedDB playlists into SQLite once using an app-state flag.

After a non-temporary playlist is added, playlist effects navigate by source type: Xtream credentials go to `/workspace/xtreams/:id`, Stalker MAC portals go to `/workspace/stalker/:id`, and M3U playlists go to `/workspace/playlists/:id`.

M3U playback state is centralized in NgRx action groups for playlists, channels, EPG, favorites, and filters; the documented module uses virtual scrolling, grouped channel views, EPG integration, and drag/drop favorites for large playlists.

The Xtream portal data boundary is `IXtreamDataSource`, covering playlist CRUD, category/content loading and saving, search, favorites, recent items, playback positions, cache clearing, content refresh, and user-data restore.

`XtreamStore` is a signal-store facade composed from portal, content, selection, search, EPG, player, favorites, recent-items, and playback-position features, and it clears per-playlist session cache during store reset.
Xtream routes live under `xtreams/:id` and split into live, VOD, series, favorites, recent, search, recently-added, and downloads child routes, with catalog facade providers on VOD and series.

Stalker routes live under `stalker/:id` and split into VOD, ITV live, series, favorites, recent, search, and downloads child routes; `StalkerStore` is a signal-store facade composed from portal, selection, content, series, player, favorites, recent, and EPG features.

The Stalker architecture document scopes support to live TV, VOD, series, VOD-as-series shapes, favorites, recently viewed, search, shared external-player playback, and live remote-control navigation.

Desktop downloads are queued one active item at a time, persisted in the `downloads` table, updated through electron-dl callbacks, broadcast to the renderer with `DOWNLOADS_UPDATE_EVENT`, and retried by re-queuing failed or canceled rows.
Repository guidance asks agents to keep TypeScript files under 300 lines, treat 350-400 lines as a hard maximum, and refactor first when new work would push a file past that range.

Repository guidance requires modern Angular patterns: signal-based queries, `input()`/`output()` instead of decorators for component IO, signal primitives for reactive state, and template control flow with `@if`, `@for`, and `@switch`.
